Welcome to Partnering for Profit. We're going to continue the conversation today and on this call we'll be focusing on the partnering idea. And we'll be looking at it really from both sides, from the copyright, the benefits to the copywriter who wants to partner instead of freelance and also with a company that would rather develop a deeper relationship with his copywriter than he's able to through freelancing. And so I hope you'll be with us for those. I think they're really great. Basically I've made the case over and over again that they say I'm the world's highest paid copywriter and I suspect that may be true. If there's somebody that's making more it's not much more and I certainly don't know who that would be but it's not because I'm the world's best copywriter. I think I write free darn good copy and I'd be more than happy to go up against pretty much anybody out there but where the extra money comes from, where the money multiplier is, both for myself and for my clients is that I don't freelance, that I develop deeper relationships with my clients and that allows my clients to grow more quickly and it allows me to make three to four times more than the most senior copywriters out there that I know about. So I want to share these ideas with you guys. For business owners on this call, I think if you give partnering a try you'll never want to go back because you'll be making more money and finding the entire process easier and much more pleasant. And also for the copywriters on the call the benefits are obvious. Not just more money but more time spent each month actually doing what you do best which is writing less time doing the things that most copywriters don't enjoy doing and don't get paid for things like lining up customers, clients, selling yourself, doing research on a new market or a new product or a new company every month. So partnership has just tremendous advantages over freelancing and it's great to have this tool in your arsenal have it available to you. And so that's what we're going to look into a little more deeply today. So let's go ahead and jump right in. I've tried to lead people through the process of entering into partnerships and it usually begins especially if you're a new copywriter with selecting the niche that you're going to work in with care. You want a niche where you're going to be writing about something that either you know about already or look forward to learning about because you're going to be immersed in that subject whether it's health or finance or bodybuilding or personal security whatever. You selected a niche that you're looking forward to writing about. The niche should also be one that has a large prospect base and is growing. As I pointed out on Tuesday there are some niches that are shrinking in size like the direct mail investment newsletter niche. That thing was oh 10 million prospects just a few years ago and now I think you'd be, I'd be surprised if there was more than a million prospects in that niche today by prospects. I mean people who are currently subscribed to other financial newsletters. So you want a niche that's growing and that's substantial to begin with. The next step is that you selected the best potential partners in that niche. You maybe went to the library and got the standard rate data service, the SRDS and you looked up the niche that you're interested in writing for and you've identified the companies that have the largest customer bases and you've researched those companies. You've looked at the demographics and psychographics of their file. You've looked at how their customers are buying products, what kind of offers they're responding to, whether they're soft offers, hard offers, continuity offers, two step offers. You've also looked at their hotline names to determine how quickly those companies are growing. You've identified or isolated a number of those companies that would be just fantastic if you could work with and you've contacted those companies and you've established a relationship with them and hopefully you've done a job or two with them just as a freelancer because most of the companies you're going to be talking to have only worked with freelancers. It's the only model they know. The last thing you want to do, and this is a really important point, the whole process of moving from a freelance career in which your income is going to be, obviously it depends on the quality of the work you do and the response you're getting. As I pointed out on Tuesday, staff copywriters typically make the average salary for staff copywriters 90,000 a year according to Yahoo Finance. The freelance copywriter, the ones that I know, are making, all making six figures. A couple of them have cracked the million dollar a year mark, but that's about it. The next level up really is the partnering level. There are only a few who are doing it. Most who are doing it are people that I've helped get started in it, people like Paris Glenpropolis and Bob Hutchinson and Jim Punkery and others. They've established partnerships with companies like Early to Rise and with Rich Sheffrin and with SoundView publications down in Atlanta. These guys are making really good money and they're on hand to stay well above the $1 million mark for a long time. Partnering is really what got me to the $4 million a year mark, and that's just with a couple of clients. It's really a great way to go. You don't walk in the door and say, hey, I want to be your partner. I want you to give me seven streams of income. I want you to pay me four times more than other copywriters. You got to do this in a way that makes sense for the client or it's not going to work. The best way really is with a first date concept where you go in and either it's a client you're already working with and you enjoy working with them and you're making money for the client and you're earning good royalties, or it's a new client that you're just now basically developing a relationship with. Allow it to be on the freelance basis because that's what they're comfortable with and go in and prove your value, create a winner or two. The next step is you broaden your field of influence within that company. As part of your research, for example, on a particular promotion, you'll ask questions about what happens to you. How did you come up with this strategy? How did you come up with this particular offer? Have you tested this? Have you tested that? What happens to the new customer after I bring him in the door with this new copy I'm going to write for you? Is there a gauntlet or a honeymoon period when he first comes on the file where you handle him differently than you do your average customer? What are the products that you're going to ultimately sell to this person? How do you sell to them? Do you do it online? Do you do it through the mail? Do you do it by phone? How do you continue to market to the people that I'm bringing in? As you have those conversations, you're going to have ideas. As you put those ideas on the table, and I recommend that you do it by asking questions Have you tried this? Have you tested that? Or saying, I was doing some research the other day and I noticed your biggest competitor does it this way. Have you tried that? Because a lot of the ideas that you have as a new writer and a fresh set of eyes on this particular company and its product are going to be ideas they've had before. Some of them are going to be ideas they've tested before and that they think didn't work, maybe. Making declarative statements, well if you do this you'll double your response, you run the risk of looking like a bit of a buffoon. It's always better just to pose it as a question and lead your client through that way. At some point during this process, in my experience at least, the client is typically the first one to raise the issue of a deeper relationship with you. Either he's going to want more access to you, he's going to want more of your writing time in a year, he's going to ask you for an exclusive maybe. He doesn't want you writing for his competitors. Or he's going to say something like, gee I wonder if you'd take a look at our package stuffers. I wish you'd take a look at our customer file marketing. When that happens, the implication is immediate. If you're writing for the front end, and we'll get more into this in a moment, your compensation is structured a particular way tailored for the fact that you're writing for the front end to attract new customers. When you begin writing other types of copy, and we'll look at six other kinds of writing projects that all of our clients need, the compensation structure that works for new customer acquisition doesn't necessarily work. The minute you begin talking about doing a different kind of copy for the client, that immediately suggests a different kind of deal with the client. That means it's time to close the deal. It's time to sit down and work out how you're going to work together moving forward. One of the key moments in this entire process is immediately after it becomes clear to the two of you that you want a deeper relationship and that you want to focus more of your time and attention on that one client, at that moment in time, you really need one-on-one face time with the client. One of the beauties of this approach of partnering really is that you don't have to live where the partner lives. You don't have to be in the same city or the same state or even in the same country. My partners, the companies I partner with, are all over the place. We get together face to face, maybe once a quarter, and do all the rest of our work over the phone and by email. You don't have to give up your dream of living where you want to live. When you're beginning this new relationship and certainly when you're discussing what kind of agreement you're going to have going forward, I like to go visit the client. I want to see his offices. I want to see him face to face. I want to look him in the eyes as I ask questions and as he negotiates with me. Mostly I want to get to know him on a personal level. You can't do that in the office. This is presupposing you're talking with the owner of the company, which I highly recommend. If it's a much larger corporation, you're not going to be, but you'll be talking to the chief decision maker in the marketing area. That's fine too. In most of the companies you'll deal with, it's the owner who's going to ultimately make the decision. I like to take him out to dinner and sit around, have some sushi, chat, get to know him. If it's a beginning of a new relationship, I like to take the redhead with me and have him bring his wife with him to dinner and just kind of relax together. During that time, it's a perfect time to pick his brain in terms of what his objectives are, what his goals are, what his frustrations are, what he thinks the obstacles to growth are in his company. You get the benefit of his experience and his intimate knowledge of his own company, his own market, his own products prior to forming your own impressions after meeting his staff, which typically will happen the next day. On one occasion, Wendy and I went on a cruise with a new client. You'll probably enjoy this. It was really as simple as, I came to this guy with an idea for a new product. He said, okay, who's going to write the promotion? I said, I will. I gave him my standard freelance rates. He says, okay, let's do it. We did it. We had about 40,000 subscriptions in a very short order, which was more than he had in his entire company at the time. We were talking on the one day and he says, wow, this is incredible. More than double the size of my company just a couple of months. I just asked him, I said, you want to take it to the next level? He just said, yeah, what do you have in mind? I said, well, I've got some ideas, but we really can't do it in a business environment. It's going to require us to sit back, do a little brainstorming, and really talk about it. Why don't we take a cruise together? We took a five-day cruise, the four of us together. It gave us plenty of time, away from the office, and interruptions, and everything, to really establish the kind of working relationship we wanted going forward. I don't necessarily recommend it. It's just something I did once and it worked out fantastic. That client's paid me millions since. Now it's time to get down to the nut cutting. It's time to begin thinking about the kind of relationship you're going to have going forward. I just put together some rules here. The goal of both parties here is to create a win-win situation. The negotiation needs to be friendly. It needs to be relaxed. Use a copywriter or use a business owner. Need to really listen to and think about what your counterparty is saying. The meaning behind his words, is he objecting to a particular clause just because he's cheap, or is it because he thinks doing that would create maybe an organizational nightmare in his company? I've had situations like that, for example, where I said, well, I want this. I want this percent of these sales I create. The client didn't say, well, you know what? That means I'm going to have to establish this whole new reporting methodology inside the company. It's going to take weeks. My IT department is driving me crazy anyway. As such a pain in the butt, isn't there a better way that we could do this? Instead of saying that, he just said, I don't know. It was really easy to assume it was just he was trying to cheap out on me. The real problem was something that was solvable, which had to do with reporting and calculating the commission. Really listen as you're going through this and try to understand why certain proposals are being made and why certain objections are being raised. Look for opportunities to minimize or eliminate the client's risk on the various types of projects you're going to be doing. If you have an ongoing relationship with a client, if you know the work is going to be there month after month, and or retainer or any of the other things that we're going to look at in a minute. Especially if you own a piece of his back end, the promotions that are going to his existing customers, you can afford to do things on the front end to save him money and lower his risk. You can ask for smaller advances. You can even do things for free as we'll see in a moment. Long as that customer base is growing and you're benefiting from the growth of the base because you're receiving a commission on back end sales. If you're a copywriter, you need to think about why in the world would any business owner want to do this? One of the reasons he wants to do it is he loves the idea of you taking a risk along with him. He loves the idea of how incentivized he thinks you're going to be. If you're guaranteed your front end advance or whatever is less and you're living more on a percentage of what you produce. He's going to love that. He's also going to love the fact that there are some things you'll do for free. I actually look for opportunities to do that. This kind of gets into the next point too, offer strategic value or freebies. I look for opportunities to do stuff for free. I was looking for a new CEO and he's running a classified ad. Why don't you let me look at that copy? Maybe I can help it a little bit. Little things that take me five or 10 minutes but mean a lot to the client. As you're going through this, there's sometimes a temptation to kind of be too clever by half in terms of negotiating particular points in the contract that never does you any good. Just as to your client, his relationship with a customer is the most important thing. As a copywriter, your relationship with that client is by far the most important thing. It's very important that you not get in a situation where you spend the time and money to negotiate a long-term agreement, a long-term partnership with a client. Then in month one, the client realizes that a clause that he agreed to doesn't really mean what he thought it was. It's very important that the client agreed to fulfill certain responsibilities to you. In other words, he's going to provide certain infrastructure. He's going to review and turn around the copy within a given period of time. Whatever the things are that you need from him, like for example, I require that my clients provide the research required in order for me to write a project. Because I may be a good copywriter but I'm not a great researcher. Just makes sense especially in the financial and health areas where the information and the research is quite technical, it just makes sense to have somebody whose whole life is doing that kind of research do the work. Make sure the client's responsibilities are clearly delineated. He understands what's going to be expected of him in order for you to give him the greatest possible value. Make sure that in your agreement and in the conversation leading up to the actual contract that your responsibilities are equally well stated. Anything that you leave vague or potentially assumed in that discussion or in the agreement is an opportunity for disappointment down the road or for misunderstanding. A great agreement also includes carrots and sticks. We'll look at a couple of them in a moment. It includes incentives for you to do your best work, incentives for the client to provide you what you need in terms of security, prompt payment, so forth. It also includes penalties. The contracts that I'm going to be sending you later today or certainly by Friday at the latest. Working on that PDF because we have five contracts I want to send you that show you five different kinds of deals that I've done. These are actual contracts, but we've had to go through and expunge any references to the actual clients and even some of the numbers associated with deal points because of confidentiality agreements I've had. The structure of the contracts are there. You'll see in there that there's maybe one clause in all of those contracts that says that if I rob a bank or do something horribly immoral, the contract can be canceled. Every single one of them has some kind of a provision that the client will pay me on a certain date or begin paying interest. Make sure that you receive that your agreement has rewards and consequences for lack of performance. That goes both ways, whether you're a business owner or the copywriter. It's also important that you discuss and have institutionalized the very foundation for the compensation for the copywriter, which is a way of measuring and reporting accurately and in a timely manner the results of the work that's been done, whether it's a customer file promotion or a new customer acquisition promotion. In some cases from time to time, clients have asked me to sit on their board of directors. That's not a good idea. There's nothing wrong with accepting an invitation to go to a meeting of the board, but the directors of a corporation have legal liability. If there's any kind of a problem with a regulating authority or any kind of a legal problem in a company, you could find yourself running up some pretty substantial legal bills. It's a good idea. My advice is just avoid that. Thank them for the compliment. Just say that I'd be more than happy to sit in any time I can help. Avoid employee status. You're going to notice in the contracts that we send you that in almost every one of them there's a very clear clause that says that this is work for hire, that you are indeed a contractor. There are a lot of reasons for that, not the least of which is that from the company's standpoint, if the IRS ever comes in and determines that you are indeed an employee, then the company is going to owe matching FICA contributions on everything they ever paid you. That's not a good thing. There'll usually be a paragraph about the fact that your status is independent contractor. Become very aware and make sure that certain financial terms are very clearly defined in the contract. You need to come to an agreement really on the difference between gross revenues, net revenues, and profit. These terms, they sound all official and if you go to accounting school they'll give you neat little definitions for them. But the fact of the matter is that every business you go into defines these things somewhat differently. We're going to look at them in greater detail in a moment, but it's very important that the two of you are on the same page in terms of these definitions. It's also very clear, very important that both the copywriter and the business owner are aware of the fact that if the compensation is based on gross revenues, it can be a smaller percentage than if it's based on net revenues or on profit. Because gross revenues, nothing is deducted net revenues, some things are deducted and on profit. Everything's deducted basically. So obviously to make the same amount of money when the base metric for your compensation changes from gross revenues to net revenues, for example, the percentage commissions that you request should change to reflect that. Okay, more rules of the road. Avoid using the instant violation trap. Not too long ago there was a guy who wrote a great book on negotiating, I can't remember the name of it, but he said his favorite technique was always negotiate contracts in which the other party was in violation of the term of the contract at the moment he signed it because that gave him power in the relationship. Some people will still use that and you need to look for it. The document that you're about to sign that you're creating as you discuss this relationship, you're going to live or die by it for a period of time. And should anything happen to the relationship, a judge is going to look at this document and say, well, you were in violation of the contract when you signed it or you became in violation at some point. So it's very important that you take these clauses in the contract seriously and to make absolutely sure if there's anything in there that you're not in compliance with, especially at the moment you sign it, that that is expunged or at least qualified. And obviously once it's signed that becomes pretty much your rule for working. So you need to stick by it. If a client asks you for exclusivity, that's an extra and there should always be a price tag attached. I will not give up exclusivity. It's a negotiating point. If a client doesn't want, in a partnership arrangement, doesn't want me also accepting a partner who's a direct competitor of his, there's going to be a concession I'm going to ask for in return for that. And it's only fair to do so because when you sign that exclusivity, it eliminates income potential for the copywriter. So it's a negotiating point. Don't let that just kind of slip in without it being discussed. Non-compete agreements I signed cheerfully. Now the difference here is that an exclusivity agreement says, okay, I won't work for your competitor. A non-compete agreement says, look, I'm not going to learn everything I could learn about your company and they'll go out for my own company to compete with you directly. And so signing non-compete agreements has never been a problem with me because I don't intend to do that in the first place. Also they're, and I'm no lawyer. So one of the things that I'm going to say several times on this call is that a good attorney needs to be a part of this process regardless of which side of this negotiation you're on. But I've heard from many, many sources, and I've never heard anyone contradicted, that most non-compete agreements are not enforceable anyway because the courts are typically will not deprive a copywriter, for example, of his right to make a living. So even if the steal fell through. Non-disclosure clauses need, I'm fine with those too. I sign them all the time, but they need to be as specific as possible. You don't want to find yourself in a situation where you mention to somebody that you work for such and such a company and that admission itself is a violation of non-disclosure. So be sure that the contract makes it very clear what it is, what kind of information it is that you are not to disclose. Typically it would be things like overall marketing strategy, it could be the media or mailing lists that are working best for the company. Those are the important things. Those are the crown jewels and they need to be outlined in the non-disclosure as specifically as possible. Okay. Always have an attorney review your agreement before you sign it. Should be obvious, but it's also tempting sometimes to skip that step because it does take a few days and slows things down, but do it. Get hard copies with live signatures. Don't just be satisfied with something that's faxed to you. Make sure that you get an actual original copy. Provide copies to your CPA or tax preparer. It's important that they know how you're compensated. Just so they'll know, especially at year end, if there are ways to save you on your taxes. We talked about listening carefully to the concerns of both parties. Some people say never pay for anything if you're the copywriter slash marketing rainmaker in this equation. I think what I like to do is I like to have my contract have provisions. If I have to come and visit, the client is going to pay my airfare and travel expenses and food expenses on the road, portal to portal. If I incur a direct cost while I'm working on one of his projects, he has to reimburse me, things like that. But then I turn around and I never bill them for travel. I never bill them for those incidental expenses. The reason for that is, again, I'm trying to, those kinds of things create a sense of loyalty, obligation, inferred obligation. They just help the bonding process with the client. If you're nickel and diming them for every phone call, I don't know if you've ever said the lawyer's office watched them work, but they have a little computer and when the phone rings, they hit their mouse and a little clock starts ticking. That client is being charged by the minute for the phone call. Obviously the first five minutes of every call is how you do and how's the weather, how's your wife, how's the kids, how about those mats? And then at the end of the call, he hits his mouse button again and off to receivables, goes his little memo, says, be sure to bill client ABC this much money. Well, that ticks people off and there's no need for it in the kind of relationship you're trying to build. So, again, any chance you can bring value for the client or demonstrate your loyalty to the company. Essentially, as a coffee writer marketing, a marketer who's partnering with a company, your mission in life now is to make that business owner more successful, richer. And your job, basically, you want him to know at every step of the way that you take a bullet form. You're there to make this happen. And this is just another way of doing that, of demonstrating that. I suggest that you work through an LLC or a corporation. This gives you some protection should the company be sued or if there's other legal problems, gives you some protection against any damaged parties coming against your personal assets. Talk to a lawyer or a good CPA about this if you're not already working through one of these kinds of organizations. They'll give you some good advice on it. I use an LLC and I don't know why. My guy says it's better than a subchapter S nowadays, so I've gone that way. This is a big one. When I went to work at Blanchard Company, the company owner said, I want to pay you on a percentage of the net, the bottom line. And I said, what are you crazy? That means that you're going to deduct overhead, which I have no control over, investment in the company, which I have no control over. Your own compensation, which I have no control over, interest on loans that you take out, which I have no control over, taxes, which I have no control over, amortization. I have no control over any of those things. The only thing I have control over is your response rate and your average unit sales. No, I'm not going to work for net. So make sure to avoid allowing these things to sneak into your compensation calculation. We already discussed this. If costs are deducted prior to your compensation being calculated, make sure your percentage is adjusted upwards accordingly. You can't always do this, but it's nice when you can. Banks still love to play the waiting game on out-of-state checks, even banks you've been with for a long time. My out-of-state clients, some of them will wire my monthly compensation to me. Others just think it's too much of a hassle, but it's really nice when you can set that up. And this is important, this next one. There's a personality type. Wendy's family is famous for it called passive aggressive. That's the personality type that sits around and endures a negative situation. And then they go home at night and they kick the dog and they yell at their spouse. But they wouldn't dream of bringing up the problem with the client. But they'll tell everyone else who'll listen what a pain in the butt the client is. I've often told people what they ought to do is just bring the client home, let the client kick the dog, cut out the middleman. But don't be passive aggressive. Be an adult. If there's a problem, confront it immediately with the client. Just call them up and say, hey, something's going on here I'm not real comfortable with. Can we talk about it for a minute? Just lay it out on the table. And next month when we do this call, we're going to be looking at the care and feeding of a client, of a partner. And some of the problems that you'll run into when you're partnering with a client. And one of those problems is dealing with the staff. And not in all cases, are they going to be thrilled that there's an outside marketing guy, or gal, an outside rain maker, who's this hero who's coming into the company really making it grow, they're going to resent you. And dealing with that resentment is extremely important. And doing it effectively is absolutely essential. And protect the relationship at all costs. Remember that this document, the very purpose of an agreement or a contract, is to make sure that everybody is on the same page so that nobody's disappointed, nobody's surprised. The purpose of this is to protect the relationship. If you're the business owner, the last thing you want is a marketer who's making you tens of millions of dollars to get pissed off and walk. Because there's 25,999,000 other small businesses out there that he can go talk to. The fact of the matter is that they desperately need his help too. If you're the copywriter and you've got a client and the chemistry's right and you're making good money and you enjoy the work, the last thing you want to do is to allow the relationship to deteriorate and lose that client. So protect the relationship at all costs. And what that means is if there's something that isn't working, if for any reason the client is, this contract shouldn't be a gotcha document. It should be preceded by at least a verbal agreement that, hey, look, we're friends, we know we work well together, we know we're making money together. We're doing this to protect the relationship, to make sure that everyone's on the same page. I quite often tell my partners, look, I love you. I trust you. There's absolutely nothing that I'm worried about in terms of getting paid here. But let's face it, you could get hit by a bus tomorrow. Somebody else could be running your company and all of a sudden my family's going hungry because they're reneging on the agreement that you and I have, so we have to have this on paper. And it's true. Those are the reasons why it's so essential to have a contract. I think it's also important too because in the early going of the relationship, when the money is small, it's not as big a deal. But when your checks get to be $100,000 a month, $200,000 a month, $500,000 a month, some companies, some clients may get a little dodgy on you and having a legal document that has recorded their obligation to you and returned for that, the growth you've created, really will help you sleep nights. I want to just move ahead. Some of these thoughts are unrelated, but I think they kind of lead us somewhere. So let's move on with it. Keep in mind that there are three kinds of promos you're going to work on. And actually, there is a fourth that I thought of later. There are going to be promotions that the copyrighter is going to work on where there's no marketing cost and no fulfillment cost. Typical of that would be an email campaign selling a product that's delivered on the web. Let's say it's an email campaign selling a $79 report on investing. So there's no marketing cost, no fulfillment cost. In this case, gross profit and net profit are the same thing, or gross revenue and net revenue are the same thing. So your percentage is very easy to calculate that. Then there will be promotions where you have marketing costs, but zero fulfillment costs. This would be like a PPC campaign. I have a client right now that is spending $350,000 a month on his PPC budget, his paper click budget online. But he's selling a product where he has no fulfillment costs. It's a digital product. In this case, that must be an investment advisory service. So these marketing costs need to be deducted prior to my commission. So my royalty rate is going to be higher in terms of a percentage on this one. There are then campaigns where you have both marketing costs and fulfillment costs, like for example with a health client who's selling supplements through the mail. He's paying $560, $600 a thousand to mail the promotion piece. And then on top of that, whenever anybody buys $100 worth of product, the client has maybe $20 or $30 worth of fulfillment costs, product and shipping costs in that. So at each of these levels here, you need to be aware, you need to have a different percentage that you're going to be working with the client. What I typically do is I will use this kind of a campaign, kind of as my bellwether, and say, well, on this kind of a campaign, I want 10%. But if you're going to deduct 10% of sales, but on this kind of campaign, if you're going to deduct marketing costs first, I'm going to need to make the same amount of money, I'm going to need a little bit higher percentage. And especially in the early, and same thing here, especially in the early going, it's always good to sit down and do a spreadsheet and kind of figure out what these costs are and how that percentage change as you're negotiating the deal. Now when you're working with your partner, you know, as a freelancer, nine times out of 10, in my career, I was hired to do a new customer acquisition promo, typically a direct mail promo or, you know, promotion, web-based promotions designed to attract new customers from cold lists. And that's the kind of work most freelancers are doing. Not hard and fast course, you know, people are all over the map with this. But I would have to guess that's probably what most people are doing. And there's one kind of compensation schedule for that, actually several we'll look at. Then there are customer file promos. Now the difference between these two is the quantity of these that go out is huge, right? But the net revenue or the profit on those kinds of promotion is very small because the response rate is much smaller because these people don't know you, right? Then there's customer file promotions. Well, on a customer file, your response rate is going to be higher because they know you and the average show is going to be higher because they trust you. And so you need to be looking at a different kind of compensation schedule there. Then there's house copy and house copy can be anything, a catalog or a website that you write once but continues producing sales forever. Package stuffers that go out every time they ship a product that are designed to sell additional product. Thank you letters, renewal series, all of those kinds of things. All of this is just, all of this is copywriting, okay? Somebody asked on Tuesday, do I need to be a marketing consultant or know all about organization and procedures and all of that kind of stuff in order to partner? And the answer is no. If you're a copywriter and you have your training like Mark does, Mark Carroll wrote a question. He says, I'm just starting the AWAI six-figure copywriting course. I don't have any clients yet. How can I get started in partnering but I don't have any copywriting experience? I think Mark, the answer to that is you need to go ahead and finish the course and you need to begin as a freelancer and pick, as we just went over, pick up a few jobs, have a few successes and the companies that you have the successes with then become your best prospects for partnering. Haroon wanted to know who paid for the cruise. I don't even remember. I think he did. I'm not sure. I think my client did. Anyway, so as a copywriter, if your only skill is copywriting and you haven't really gotten into the nuts and bolts of marketing, you can still partner doing the first three things on this list. If, however, you've added to your inventory of tools and you understand the nuts and bolts of marketing and you understand strategy and tactics, you can also get involved in marketing consultation, which is very nice, where you go in and you give them ideas for creating new products or new offers or new marketing channels or media. You should be paid for that. That can be a very lucrative area. Actually, it's lucrative because there is no directly measurable number of dollars generated from that. I'll show you why that can be so lucrative in a moment. Then there's organizational and procedural consulting. The more you deal with a company, the more you're going to become aware of situations in the company that are obstacles to its success. I call them choke points. Maybe it's organized upside down. Maybe the marketing people don't have enough authority and the financial people have way too much. Maybe the head of the marketing department needs to be the assistant head of the marketing department. You need to bring in somebody who's sharper. Maybe the procedures that they're using are slowing things down. We've talked before about the fact that income is measured in time. How much do you make? I make this much per year. A year has to be there or the amount is meaningless. Since income and therefore company growth is measured in terms of time, that means that every day that a promotion is delayed this year, that means one more promotion is pushed one day farther into next year at the end of the chain. The thing that slows down the marketing push is costing that company money. It's costing that company growth and it's costing you money this year. Turning things around quickly, a good example of that is in DirectMill where you have a number of customer acquisition promotions per year. Maybe you can get eight of them done per year. But if you could do nine, you're going to bring in that many more customers this year. The company will grow that much faster. On the other hand, if you do seven, it has a dramatic effect on the company's growth. Anything to speed that process or to make it more efficient so that the process costs the client less, it doesn't have to hire as many people, that kind of thing, has tremendous value for the customer and for your client. Again, simply because there's no way to really track the dollar amount that you make your client when you're doing these things, prevents you with a tremendous opportunity as a copywriter, marketer, partner. Then finally, there's deal making, especially once you've been in the market for a while. You may contact other companies. You may know vendors. You may be able to go to your client and say, look, I've got some time on my hands here. I was just thinking if we took that campaign we did to our house file last month, and if we gave it to this company over here and let him run it to his house file, we could probably get half the money and the names and addresses of all the people that buy it. The marketing's already done. Why the heck not? If he likes that idea, say, well, look, why don't I give you a list of 10 companies I'd like to approach with that deal? You should be compensated for those kinds of things. Those are the six kinds of work that you're going to be doing most often. Let's see if I can get this bad boy to change. Within that, there's seven income streams, seven ways to get paid. The first one is royalties. Royalties have to do with paying you per number of eyeballs to see your promotion. Direct mail, for example, new customer acquisition promotions. Direct mail royalties are based on the number of pieces mailed. If you write a promotion, you can get, say, five cents for every copy of your promotion that's delivered through the mail. It's based on the quantity of promotions that are sent. Number two is commissions. This is a compensation that's based on the amount of money you produce. As I pointed out earlier, if you're doing customer acquisition, especially through the mail, the cost here is very high. Every dollar you add to that client's cost means there are fewer pieces he can mail at break even to generate new customers. The royalty there, because he's not really making any money, he's trying to break even to create a new customer. It would be crazy for a copywriter to work for a percentage of profits. It would be crazy for a business owner to pay the copywriter on a basis of a percentage of revenues. Royalties make sense, but when you're brought into a house about to an existing customer, commissions make all the sense in the world. The copywriter would simply take a percentage of the sales he produces, or in some cases, a larger percentage of the increase in the sales that he produces each month. There are overrides. I've done deals, for example, where I come in on the front end and I create new customers, but Dan Rosenthal is doing the back end marketing. I said, hey, look guys, okay, I'm creating all these customers for my $50 a thousand, and I'm making a good living doing that, but I can't help but notice that Dan is then going to those people, and he's making four times what I'm making by selling to the back end. I know how much Dan's making, so I want a 5% overwrite on those back end sales on the people that I produce for you. The client says, okay, not a problem, only fair. Retainers. I've had quite a few companies over the last 15 years or so put me on retainers. They're really nice, especially if you're doing consulting work, again, that can't be tracked in terms of dollars produced. So retainer is a great way to go, $10,000, $15,000 a month that is essentially a guarantee against billable hours that you spend giving advice, learning more about the client's company, creating better procedures, making recommendations on organization, and so forth. And even, although I wouldn't recommend it necessarily, have to do with product development, things like that, because ultimately, if you're doing product development, consulting, you're going to get paid because you're going to end up doing the promotions. And there are hourly fees, you know, without a retainer, you can just say, look, tell you what, I'll do this consulting project with you, but I want $250 an hour or whatever your rate is. Another way is to have bonuses based on metrics, where you just say, look, if I can lower your marketing costs as much, or if I can lower your lead costs as much, or if I can add this many new people to your file in this period of time, would you be willing to pay a bonus? And then finally, it's equity interest. And on the equity interest side, essentially, the way it's most commonly done is with stock options. And you say, okay, well, today it's a $100 million company, all right? And I'm going to be paid directly for the new customer acquisition stuff I do. I'm going to be paid directly for the stuff I do, selling stuff to your house value, your existing customers, right? But there's a lot of other things that I'm doing for you, and if you want me to continue doing them, I'm going to need some kind of compensation. So what I'd like to do is a stock option. So you've got a $100 million company, and you've got a million shares of stock out there valued at $100 a share at today's level, right? Well, what I want is an agreement that three years from now I could buy your stock and instantly resell it to you for $100 a share. That way if I double or triple the stock's value, the value of the company during that period of time, when I do that, I'll make all the compensation I really need. Business owners don't really mind it because you're not a voting stockholder. You don't own stock, you just have an option to buy stock with an agreement that you'll immediately sell it back. I mean, it's the same meeting. Copywriters like it because you don't have to actually buy the stock either. All you have to do is execute the agreement, and it all just happens on paper. If you want to, you can hand them a check for $100,000. They'll hand you a check for $400,000, and you'll both be on your way. It offsets. So it's a really good way to go. And this made me some great money over the years. Also business owners love it because they get three years of services in the consulting area without having to really cut you a check for that time, but they get the benefit of growth that you provide for them. And one of the contracts that I'm sending you has that. Also, keep in mind that there are some times to work for free and to be really happy about it. I know that a little bit earlier, but work for free to create an unspoken sense of obligation. It's just another way to say, bond, to tighten the relationship that the two of you have together. And if you're a company owner and you're working with a copywriter marketer and he does something really good for you, do something really good for him. Just some little cheap thing, but acknowledge the fact that, wow, this guy's a fantastic team member. Marty Edelson at boardroom sends out balloon bouquets when you get a control. If a person is publishing every time I showed up, they had a billboard in those lobbies, welcome Clayton Makepeace. And sometimes they'd have on their 30 million pieces mailed or whatever the number was. And then they'd have a special brunch just in my honor. Those things mean a lot to copywriters, to marketing people to know that they're appreciated. The fact that you're working at distance from each other, it's really important to do those kinds of things, keep that relationship strong. And also remember, when you own the back end, if you're making a commission on sales to customers, anything that you do to increase the size of the customer base, you're going to eventually get paid for. And so you can afford to cut your fees dramatically. And here at Response Inc., for example, we've cut, you know, in the old days, let's say I'm working with a client on a freelance basis, he wants me to write a new customer acquisition promo for him. He's going to have to pay me a $25,000 advance and $50 a thousand. And you know, that's just the way it is. But if I own a piece of this back end, you know, I'm willing to put together a front end promotion for the guy to bring in lots more customers that I can then sell to for a fraction of that. And it just basically cover my cost of doing it, you know, and I'd be more happy to do that because I know next month, the month after, I'm going to have 5,000 more, 10,000 more, 20,000 more people to sell to on the back end. And that's where the money is, that's where the response is, and that's where the average sale is. There's options for new customer acquisition programs. You can do it free, as I just said. You can do them on an hourly fee if it's a small project. You can do them on a flat fee. I would never do that, but it's not a bad way to get started. Then there are advances against royalties, like I said, I was just explaining. And then the top, the pinnacle here is the fee plus royalty. On the advance plus royalty, the advance is deducted prior to you getting your first royalty payment with fee, royalties begin immediately. And we've actually really covered these, but there are a couple of points here I want to make. Number one is, royalty possibilities could be cents for impression, which is a standard direct mail royalty, you know, $50 per thousand or whatever. But let's say you're doing print ads. Those guys I know, if they're doing a print ad, will just take a percentage of the media budget. They'll say, okay, well, if you spend $100,000 advertised, you know, giving my copy out there, then I want a, you know, 5% or 10% of that amount to compensate me for writing the copy. And then there are these other things that are pretty self-explanatory, other ways to do it. And for two existing customers, percentage of sales or percentage of increase. And we talked about metric-based bonuses. I put these in basically so that you can kind of flip through them quickly when you're doing your deals. And I think they're all pretty self-explanatory. But you'll see I have compensation options for various types of deals that you'll do. The magic number is 10%. That's the basis that I work with. And I came up with it because in the mail, my royalty is typically $50 a thousand. A direct mail was costing $500 a thousand. So I was getting 10% of the royalty. And it works. That's where I start from. Now, as I said, if they're going to be deducting expenses from that, then the number goes up. And it's important to remember that it's my magic number, but it's not sacred. If a guy's got a particularly huge market or huge database, I will be willing to negotiate that down. I gave you some revenue definitions here that will help you. I'm not an accountant, so these are not official, but these are the kinds of definitions that I use with my clients. And they should help you through the process of negotiating this. And I think that's it. So let's see. What kind of question? Oh, there are contractual necessities here that I've also given you a checklist for. And I've pretty much covered most of those. OK, good. So Jackie wants to know during the initial conversations how we record decisions that are made. Just with a yellow pad. Both of you just sit there, talk them out, make the notes. And then I formalize them in a document to send them back. And then once we have a final draft, we're both happy with it. Go see an attorney. April says, would I stay away from companies that don't have a marketing department? It depends, April. I've worked with companies without marketing departments and had good successes with them. But it just takes a little longer because as you begin to grow them, you are going to have to address the staff problem. And Mark Carroll wants to know about what size businesses I target. Well, I'm not too concerned about the size of the business today. I'm concerned about the size of the prospect base. And I'm concerned that they have enough customers so that I can move in quickly, which is one of the things that we're going to talk about next time, how to create a million-dollar royalty for yourself in the first month, working with a new partner. That they have a sizable enough customer base that I can do that. So I hope that helps, guys. So again, thank you guys for attending. And I'll see you next time. Goodbye.
